Dr Dale Read, Senior Lecturer in Sports Performance, Institute of Sport, Manchester Met has written England’s Round of 16 World Cup clash against Mexico at the iconic Estadio Azteca presents a challenge beyond tactics and technical ability. One of the most significant factors the team will need to contend with is the effect of altitude on player performance The stadium, located in Mexico City, sits approximately 7,220 feet (2,200 metres) above sea level. At this elevation, the reduced barometric pressure means fewer oxygen molecules are available with each breath. As a result, less oxygen reaches the bloodstream and working muscles, For elite footballers, the consequences can be significant. Research has consistently shown that at altitudes above 5,000 feet, maximum aerobic capacity can decline by around five to ten per cent. In practical terms, this means players may struggle to maintain the same level of p When oxygen availability is reduced, players can fatigue more quickly and require longer recovery periods after intense periods of play. This could lead to lower overall running distances and potentially influence the tempo of the game The altitude factor may be particularly problematic for England because of the tournament schedule. Athletes competing at altitude would normally arrive between one and two weeks before competition to allow the body time to acclimatise. During this adaptation process, the body be

If you ask someone who travels across Manchester every day whether commuting has changed over the past few years, they might struggle to point to one defining moment There wasn’t a single new tram line, one piece of technology or one policy that transformed how the city moves. Instead, the changes have happened quietly, almost without people noticing. A different route to work. A decision to leave the car at home once or twice a week. A later These aren’t dramatic lifestyle overhauls. They’re small decisions, made for practical reasons, that have slowly reshaped everyday travel As work patterns continue to evolve and people become more conscious of both time and wellbeing, Manchester’s commuters are proving that lasting change often happens one habit at a time Whether it was a quick trip to the supermarket, a visit to the gym or a short journey to meet friends, reaching for the car keys required very little thought. Convenience almost always won For journeys of only a couple of miles, many people now pause before starting the engine. Fuel costs, parking charges and traffic all play a part, but they aren’t the whole story. People are becoming more aware that some short journeys can actually feel easier without the car

title: More than a game: football's role in one family's fostering journey - Rochdale Borough Council
summary: A foster family from Heywood have opened up about how their love for football has helped to create the ultimate loving environment for the children in their care
lead: A foster family from Heywood have opened up about how their love for football has helped to create the ultimate loving environment for the children in their care
practical_angle: Учитывать как новую фазу городской политики или public-affairs повестки сегодня.
raw_evidence: A foster family from Heywood have opened up about how their love for football has helped to create the ultimate loving environment for the children in their care Lauren (46) and her husband Danny Sullivan (52) have fostered for Rochdale Borough Council since 2012 and currently care for 5 young lads aged 12 to 21, alongside their 9-year-old son, Daniel Their home is very busy as you can imagine and ‘the washing machine is non-stop’ Lauren jokes. Each child has their own interests, including things like the gym and gaming, but football is what stands out as the cement in their daily routines From training and coaching on weekdays to refereeing and playing matches at the weekend, the house is football mad and it’s their passion for the sport which connects them as a family and what has helped the lads to create a strong brotherly bond “They’ll all come home after school, college or work, and Daniel will ask them for a game,” Lauren said, with a pair of football nets visible outside from the living room “It’s so lovely to see the smiles on their faces when they’re all playing together in the back garden.”

practical_angle: Учитывать как новую фазу городской политики или public-affairs повестки сегодня.
raw_evidence: The Labour Party could benefit most from the return of the Supplementary Votes (SV) system in the Greater Manchester mayoral race, a polling expert has said Voters in the region head to the polls on 30 July and will be able to select a first and second choice of candidate as SV comes into replace the first-past-the-post (FPTP) system used at the last election A number of candidates have already declared for the contest , triggered after Andy Burnham's successful campaign to become the MP of Makerfield, leaving the post he has held since 2017 vacant Prof Jonathan Tonge of the University of Liverpool said Labour could pick up second preference votes from Greens and Lib Dems, while Reform could be less likely to see Restore voters put them as second preference due to "bad blood" between the party leaders The system was used in the first two mayoral elections, but was scrapped by the last Conservative government in favour of FPTP system, before it was reintroduced again by Labour Under SCV, candidates need to win more than 50% of the vote in the first round to be come mayor or the top two candidates go into a run-off where second preference votes from eliminated candidates are counted

True crime series 'Life means life': The infamous Greater Manchester killers told they will die in prison 'Just punishment in your case requires you to be kept in prison for the rest of your life' View 12 Images Whole life orders have been around sinc News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 06:04, 04 Jul 2026 "I do not think that either of these prisoners should ever be released from custody," Margaret Thatcher wrote to her then Home Secretary Leon Brittan in 1985. "Their crime was the most hideous and cruel in modern times." Thatcher was referring to Moors Murderers Ian Brady and Myra Hindley . And five years later her wish came true In 1990 Hindley became the first woman in England and Wales to be given a whole-life term, 24 years after she and Brady were first sentenced to life in prison for the sexual abuse, torture and murder of three children. She joined a relatively small number of criminals serving the Whole life orders have been around since 1983. The Sentencing Council says they are for only the most serious cases of murder There were 74 whole-life prisoners in England and Wales as of September 2025, according to Ministry of Justice statistics. Several other inmates have died while serving a whole-life term

Money The 12 Greater Manchester businesses that went into administration in June Below is the full list of Greater Manchester businesses who called in administrators in June Comments News Charlotte Fisher Reporter 16:52, 04 Jul 2026 View Image A total A total of 12 companies across Greater Manchester were forced to call in administrators in June - including a dentist and a real estate business More than 100 companies in total across the region have been to call in administrators so far this year, with the number of companies affected peaking in January at 45 The latest figures for Greater Manchester come after it was announced that the number of registered company insolvencies across England and Wales was 1,868 in May in total, 10pc lower than in April, and 16pc lower than in May 2025. In Greater Manchester, 12 companies called in ad Tom Russell, President of R3, the UK’s restructuring, insolvency and turnaround trade body, explained: "A company is insolvent when it doesn’t have enough money to pay its debts or outgoings when they are due "One option open to an insolvent company is to enter administration. This involves the appointment of a licensed insolvency practitioner as administrator to take control of the business

title: Convicted murderer pleads guilty to kidnapping girl, 15, at knifepoint in Altrincham
summary: The incident happened on Bradgate Road last month Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A convicted murderer has pleaded guilty to kidnapping a 15-year-old girl at knifepoint and subjecting her to an eight-hour ordeal in his car Paul Sharpe, 56, of Portwood, Stockport, entered guilty
lead: The incident happened on Bradgate Road last month Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A convicted murderer has pleaded guilty to kidnapping a 15-year-old girl at knifepoint and subjecting her to an eight-hour ordeal in his car Paul Sharpe, 56, of Portwood, Stockport, entered guilty
practical_angle: Полиция или суд сообщили новое развитие по делу.
raw_evidence: The incident happened on Bradgate Road last month Share on WhatsApp Share via Email A convicted murderer has pleaded guilty to kidnapping a 15-year-old girl at knifepoint and subjecting her to an eight-hour ordeal in his car Paul Sharpe, 56, of Portwood, Stockport, entered guilty pleas at Minshull Street Crown Court on Thursday to six offences: kidnapping, sexual assault, engaging in sexual activity in the presence of a child, making threats with a bladed article, committing an offence with intent to Sharpe approached the girl on Bradgate Road in Altrincham at around 7.30pm on May 18, threatened her with a knife, put her in a headlock and forced her into his car. He told her that her father owed him money He drove her to a car park near Manchester Airport, where he tied her wrists and ankles with a necktie. Over the following hours he moved between different car parks, forcing her to drink alcohol and sexually assaulting her The girl’s mother reported her missing, prompting a major police investigation. Extensive CCTV enquiries identified Sharpe’s vehicle, and he was arrested in a hotel car park in Knutsford at around 3.40am the following morning with the girl still in the car

A man has described the "amazing" moment Pele and his Brazil teammates visited his hometown in Cheshire The South American side stayed at a hotel in Lymm during their 1966 World Cup campaign, which saw them dumped out of the competition at the group stage But as part of their time in the rural village, the squad, which also included Garrincha and Jairzinho, met with locals and signed autographs Sixty years on, Laurence Bennett, who was just five years old at the time, said he would spend mornings before school serving toast to the players as his mum Bessie worked at the Lymm Hotel "I got to just wander around these magnificent footballers," Bennett told BBC Radio Manchester He said the access people in the village had to the players would never be allowed today

More than 2,000 people will parade through Manchester later in a tradition begun by the city's Italian community nearly 140 years ago The Madonna Del Rosario procession is part of a display of religious faith but also a celebration of Italian identity with pride of place given to a 5ft (1.5m) statue of the Virgin Mary dating back nearly 100 years It begins at 13:00 BST in George Leigh Street, Ancoats, an area once known as Little Italy due to the Italian immigrants who settled there The procession, which began in 1888, leaves Ancoats with association officials and civic dignitaries at its head, led by the Lord Mayor of Manchester and the Bishop of Salford The procession involves the carrying of religious emblems – The Madonna, The Calvary, St Anthony and banners – from Ancoats across Manchester city centre, passing the Town Hall into St Peter's Square around 14:30 After a pause it returns back to George Leigh Street to finish with a short blessing outside the former St Michael's Church

Northern Quarter 'We don't need to turn the lights on at night, we just open up the curtains' The 'overbearing' digital display is to be taken down following a court battle News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 07:19, 05 Jul 2026 View Image The advertising ho A huge digital advertising hoarding in the Northern Quarter which could cause a 'severe' distraction for drivers is to be taken down following a court battle The 'overbearing' and 'intrusive' 19mx5m display on the busy crossroads of Rochdale Road and Addington Street was said to be 'considerably at odds' with the surrounding architecture. The massive billboard, which displays a changing roll of adverts, will now have to removed by Aug It came after Manchester council issued a 'discontinuation notice' on the land ordering the hoarding to be taken down. The council said the 'dominating' hoarding was 'contrary to the overall aims of the regeneration strategies for New Cross, NOMA and the Northern gateway' Because the illuminated sign required 'increased cognitive demand' it also had the potential to cause a 'severe' distraction for drivers and be 'detrimental' to safety, a council inspector said. The Appeal Court heard planning permission for the hoarding was first granted in Sept In 2021 it was replaced by the current digital billboard. A council inspector who visited the site in 2024 described the 'very large' billboard as 'intrusive' and 'overbearing'

Wigan The Latic fanatic, the killer driver and the family left to pick up the pieces After repeatedly running Darryl Tomlinson over, Megan Murphy stepped out of the car, lied to police then called a pal to say she had run over a 'smackhead'. In an int It was 7am when Darryl Tomlinson’s family got the phone call. Mum, Michelle, and Dad, Paul ‘Tommy’ Tomlinson, were celebrating her 50th birthday in Tenerife What followed was unimaginable horror, shock and a long, long journey home Over 12 hours later they arrived back in Wigan to learn that their beloved son had been hit by a car and lost his life. Initially, Paul's instinct was to forgive his son's killer Then he learned the truth of her callous actions In the moments that followed the incident, Megan Murphy lied to the police about what she had done. And as Darryl lay dying, trapped under her car, she called a pal and branded her victim a 'smackhead'

The massive billboard, which displays a changing roll of adverts, will now have to removed by Aug It came after Manchester council issued a 'discontinuation notice' on the land ordering the hoarding to be taken down. The council said the 'dominating' hoarding was 'contrary to the overall aims of the regeneration strategies for New Cross, NOMA and the Northern gateway' Because the illuminated sign required 'increased cognitive demand' it also had the potential to cause a 'severe' distraction for drivers and be 'detrimental' to safety, a council inspector said. The Appeal Court heard planning permission for the hoarding was first granted in Sept In 2021 it was replaced by the current digital billboard. A council inspector who visited the site in 2024 described the 'very large' billboard as 'intrusive' and 'overbearing'

Wigan The Latic fanatic, the killer driver and the family left to pick up the pieces After repeatedly running Darryl Tomlinson over, Megan Murphy stepped out of the car, lied to police then called a pal to say she had run over a 'smackhead'. In an int It was 7am when Darryl Tomlinson’s family got the phone call. Mum, Michelle, and Dad, Paul ‘Tommy’ Tomlinson, were celebrating her 50th birthday in Tenerife What followed was unimaginable horror, shock and a long, long journey home Over 12 hours later they arrived back in Wigan to learn that their beloved son had been hit by a car and lost his life. Initially, Paul's instinct was to forgive his son's killer Then he learned the truth of her callous actions In the moments that followed the incident, Megan Murphy lied to the police about what she had done. And as Darryl lay dying, trapped under her car, she called a pal and branded her victim a 'smackhead'

Employment Two very different corners of Greater Manchester, one very similar problem SPECIAL INVESTIGATION Youth unemployment is rising across the country, and The Manchester Evening News has delved into the latest figures View 7 Images A tale of two News Stephen Topping Senior reporter and Richard Ault 07:11, 05 Jul 2026 The numbers are stark. More than one million under-25s across the country are not in education, employment or training according to the latest official figures Greater Manchester is no different. Unemployment is rising among young adults and it's a problem of which political leaders are only too aware Both Sir Keir Starmer and his likely-successor Andy Burnham have been discussing ways to tackle it over recent days. But the problem isn't straightforward The causes of rising youth unemployment appear to be varied - and the end result is being seen in communities with very different demographics The Manchester Evening News has delved into the latest figures from the House of Commons Library which show the number of people aged 18 to 24 who are claiming either Jobseeker’s Allowance or Universal Credit with a requirement to seek work. The data is broken down into parliamen

A company and its director have been found guilty of illegally supplying specialist medical equipment for feeding newborn babies in hospital critical care settings around the world Medicina Ltd and Kenneth Harrison, from Bolton, were convicted of several fraud offences relating to feeding pumps and tubes for babies as well as blunt fill needle devices used to draw up and prepare fluids and medications The company managed to get its devices into hospitals in the UK, Spain, Australia and New Zealand The successful prosecution came after a complex 13-year investigation, although Medicina has said the offences took place under different management The fraud unravelled after the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) first identified concerns in 2013 Its investigation found a number of devices, including enteral feeding pumps and gastrostomy "button" devices used to provide feed directly into the stomach of very sick newborn babies, were supplied with CE markings that were not valid

A man who had both his legs amputated in 2024 after getting an infection has become a catwalk model in a bid to inspire others Michael Hopkinson, from Manchester, was among the models who were showcasing the latest line from Recondition - a brand set up to create adaptive clothing The 39-year-old said he wanted other people to be proud of their prostheses "People have a lot of shame about prosthetics and not showing them and even if just one person who is going through amputations sees this and goes actually... I'm going through this and I can relate," he said Hopkinson added: "That's how I think it works - it's all about relatability, and this is a very good opportunity to do that." Models travelled from across the country to take part in the fashion show at the Aviva Studios in Manchester

Campaigners, who have been protesting for years about trees being chopped down at a beauty spot, are now working with the council on how to carry out the safety works needed A new proposal, drawn up by the Friends of Poynton Pool and Cheshire East Council, follows the current footpath and is designed to "avoid as many trees as possible" The campaign group and the authority said they had "agreed a preferred way forward" and the new proposals included "relatively minor engineering works" Cheshire East Council first outlined plans to remove trees at the ornament pond, which dates back to 1750, in 2023, sparking demonstrations outside Macclesfield Town Hall Because of its size, Poynton Pool is legally classed as a reservoir As a result, the council said it was required to do safety works on the site

Police have warned people watching England's World Cup clash with Mexico that it will be carrying out roadside drink-driving tests on Monday The game was due to kick off at 01:00 BST in Mexico City, and some pubs and bars in the region were expected to stay open late to allow fans to watch Thomas Tuchel's men attempt to win a place in the quarter-finals A spokesman for Greater Manchester Police said: "With the match taking place later in the evening, some people may continue drinking into the early hours "While you may feel fit to drive the following morning, alcohol can remain in your system for longer than you think and you could still be over the legal limit." On Friday, the National Police Chiefs Council criticised the timing of the government's decision to let pubs stay open until 05:00 BST on Monday It said the "late announcement" meant officers would have to be taken away from other duties and work longer hours, even though the team's likely route through the tournament "has been known for a considerable time"

An 18-year-old man is in a critical condition in hospital after reports that his off-road bike struck a stationary car, police said Officers then arrested two men on suspicion of obstructing them from helping the man, who had crashed on St Helens Road, Bolton, at about 22:18 BST on Saturday A force spokesperson the man was given first aid before he was taken to hospital with serious injuries, where he remains in a critical condition The two men were arrested on suspicion of obstructing police and a Section 5 Public Order Act offence Tell us which stories we should cover in Greater Manchester Contact form Contact form Listen to the best of BBC Radio Manchester on Sounds and follow BBC Manchester on Facebook , external , X , external , and Instagram , external . Styal Prison Prison officer brushed it off as a migraine, then she woke up to 'terrifying' experience Prison officer Tia Bradbury returned to the frontline at HMP Styal after undergoing brain surgery Comments News Amy Walker Court reporter 05:33, 06 J When Tia Bradbury started experiencing a headache and had difficulty seeing at 25, she brushed it off as just another migraine. She had no idea it was a sign of something much more sinister At the time, Tia’s eldest child was just one year old – and she was 22 weeks pregnant with her second. Then the young mother woke up one morning to find the vision in one of her eyes had disappeared completely "It was terrifying," the now 27-year-old recalls "I suffered from migraines, so at first I thought it was just another one,” she said. “But my sight didn’t come back. One morning I woke up and I couldn’t see out of one eye at all." After visits to an optician and hospital, scans revealed the problem - a 3cm brain tumour

Rochdale 'I was terrified as my rapist ex tried to appeal his conviction, but now I'm free' 'For anybody reading this and thinking they aren't strong enough to leave or to go through the legal process, you are' View 4 Images Speaking out: Hannah Mello News Paul Britton Reporter 05:32, 06 Jul 2026 A mum who was raped and abused by her then partner in a sickening campaign of violence and threats over years says she finally has justice Hannah Mellor spoke out after top judges at the Court of Appeal in London refused Ben Fitton's bid to appeal against his conviction and sentence The 33-year-old - who has bravely waived her right to anonymity to give hope to others in the same position she was in - told today how Fitton's appeal meant he was 'still in the shadows' of her life for 17 long months She admitted to being 'terrified' leave to appeal would be granted, but said the court's decision meant that, finally, she has peace Hannah revealed her mother travelled down to London to attend Thursday's appeal hearing in person, without her knowledge

Glossop Glossop homes evacuated and roads cordoned off after 'item dug up' in garden Multiple police vehicles were seen as roads in Glossop were taped off News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 20:06, 05 Jul 2026 Updated 21:44, 05 Jul Businesses and homes were reportedly evacuated with roads cordoned off amid what is understood to be an 'explosives'-related incident in Glossop on Sunday (July 5) Multiple police cars were seen along High Street West following the incident which is thought to have occurred nearby on Chapel Street. Witnesses reported that local residents along with nearby businesses were temporarily evacuated by emergency crews after the alarm was raised One man who found the item while gardening said the incident was escalated by emergency crews with explosives specialists also called out to the scene. It has been suggested the item was a 'world war two' device Derbyshire Constabulary later confirmed ordnance, believed to be from the second world war, was discovered and that the Explosive Ordnance Disposal Unit teams had been dispatched to the scene to assess it View 3 Images Cordons were put in place (Image: Louis Ashurst)

Mental health An army career turned his life around - then it unravelled Gary Hind's anxiety led to physical pain that saw him physically floored News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 19:16, 05 Jul 2026 View Image Army veteran Gary Hind saw his life sp An army veteran whose military career 'turned his life around' was left physically floored as his anxiety spiralled. Gary Hind, from Hyde , lived with mental health difficulties from childhood following a troubled home situation and the sudden death of his father But the 66-year-old managed to get his life firmly on track with a career in the army. Gary joined the armed forces aged 16, and he credits the military for shaping the man he became In 1999, he received an MBE in recognition of his service. Gary went on to enjoy a new career, until events took a turn for the worse - leading to a mental health spiral that saw him suffer tightness in his chest, heart flutters and breathlessness After a difficult start in life, Gary says the army 'turned his life around'. He said: “Being in the army rebuilt me as a person. I thrived and learnt a lot of discipline and control. I felt like a new person.” When his military career came to an end, Gary found a job he loved helping students with their personal development skills and extra-curricular activities. But after 15 years in the role, a difficult period at work left him feeling unsettled and unsupported

World Cup Police stopping drivers across Greater Manchester after England v Mexico World Cup clash Punters were able to watch the World Cup match in pubs until 5am Comments News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 19:11, 05 Jul 2026 Upda Police have announced they are stopping drivers across Greater Manchester on Monday (July 6) following the early-hours England v Mexico World Cup clash The Three Lions beat Mexico 3-2 at the Azteca stadium in a game which kicked off at 2am UK time. It was announced last week that pubs would be able to stay open until 5am to host and stream the match to punters But in a new warning issued before the match, Greater Manchester Police said that officers would be conducting stops of vehicles across the region to check for any potential drug or drink driving offences after a night watching the match Due to the match taking place at the Azteca Stadium in Mexico City, the match has a later start time than other matches have done and it meant that its kick-off did not fall into the originally allowed rules of when pubs could extend their opening hours until And despite rumours FIFA might bring the kick off forward to 7pm on Sunday night, it was later confirmed kick off would remain at 1am on Monday morning - which was later put back to 2am due to thunderstorms

Salford Hundreds of people were meant to be living here by now - instead it looks like a 'bad day in Beirut' 'It's been abandoned and we've been abandoned' News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 18:42, 05 Jul 2026 View 2 Images The empty plot of land on Robert By now this desolate plot of wasteland should have been home to hundreds of people living on tree-lined streets on a brand new eco-friendly estate Instead it looks, in the words of one local resident, like a 'bad day in Beirut'. Questions are being asked over delays in the building of more than 270 homes in the Salford district of Ordsall in a scheme being managed by the council's development company Dérive The plans, which include 100 much-needed affordable houses in what is still one of the city's most deprived areas, were first approved in March 2024. It was hoped the 'vibrant, inclusive' estate's first residents would have moved in by now But more than two years on not a single brick has been laid. The two plots off Robert Hall Street were once home to a school and a shopping precinct But they have been fenced off and left to stand empty ever since they cleared of trees and groundworks carried out in the summer of 2024. And in the meantime residents say the site is becoming more and more of an eyesore

Chorlton 'If I'm going to do something this big, I want it to mean something beyond me' Toni wants the drastic change to shine a light on the community she strives to support Comments News Emma Gill Parents editor 17:05, 05 Jul 2026 View 4 Images Toni For Toni Toner her locks have been part of her identity for 14 years, so in deciding to go for 'the big chop' she was determined it would make a big difference As CEO for children and young people at Barlow Moor Community Association (BMCA) in Chorlton , she's spent years helping residents on the surrounding Merseybank Estate and Arrowfield Estate, formerly Nell Lane And while times have always been tough for many people there, she says in recent years, it's become harder than ever With demand for services soaring and voluntary sector funding harder to come by, she decided to do something drastic and have her long locks cut off to kickstart a summer of fundraising for BMCA and raise awareness of the key role such places play "The voluntary sector is really struggling; demand is at its highest while securing core funds has become increasingly difficult," said Toni, who lives in Sale Moor. "That reality is what pushed me to do something bold that could raise awareness as well as funds

Salford RSPCA responds after social media video shows dog chained up in Salford WARNING: This article contains images some readers may find upsetting News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 16:32, 05 Jul 2026 View 2 Images The RSPCA has The RSPCA has responded after a social media video appeared to show a dog trying to escape out of a chain in a yard in Salford . A clip, shared online, appeared to show the white dog trying to wriggle out of the collar with claims made that it was 'kept chained up at a scrapyard' A post by dog trainer and animal welfare campaigner Sammy Wilkinson received hundreds of responses and comments after it was shared on Thursday (July 2). "I've just watched with my own eyes this dog nearly choke himself to death trying to escape the chain," he wrote The clip, filmed in Swinton , zoomed in to show the animal appearing to be repeatedly struggling and wrestling with the collar and chain. It was claimed that the incident had been reported to the RSPCA However, a further update on social media then claimed that the owners had 'agreed to let the dog go if we can find a suitable home for him.' In a response, the RSPCA said it was 'aware of the situation' but could not comment on individual complaints or what action may or may not have been taken. It went on to encourage members of the public to report any other concerns about animals and their welfare

Manchester weather Met Office latest on when another heatwave will hit Greater Manchester It's going to be hot once again News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 15:23, 05 Jul 2026 View Image Another heatwave is on the way (Image: Met Office) If you're glad to be cooling down after the last heatwave , brace yourself for what lies ahead this week. Another heatwave is hitting the UK - and it is due in Greater Manchester before next weekend The Met Office is forecasting plenty of sunshine across the region this week. Temperatures will be more mild at the beginning of the week, before ramping up as the days go on Highs of 22C are currently forecast on Monday (July 6) and Tuesday. An overcast start to Monday should make way for sunny intervals from late morning, continuing throughout the rest of the day, with Tuesday following a similar pattern While some parts of the country are already experiencing another heatwave at the start of the week, Greater Manchester is due to warm up from Wednesday. Highs of 27C are forecast for Wednesday, with 29C on both Thursday and Friday, and 28C on Saturday - with clear skies forecast Overnight temperatures will be above average throughout the week, although they are thankfully not expected to reach 20C like in the June heatwave. Lows of between 14C and 16C are forecast over the next three nights, while they could reach 18C later in the week

Metrolink Metrolink passengers braced for three weeks of disruption with lines to close Passengers across the region will be affected News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 11:15, 05 Jul 2026 View 2 Images Improvement works are taking place on the Metro Tram passengers are braced for three weeks of major disruption as three Metrolink lines will close for improvement works. Transport for Greater Manchester (TfGM) says works are due to take place at Deansgate-Castlefield, Trafford Bar and along the Eccles line As a result, no trams will run on the Eccles, Altrincham or Trafford Centre lines. Services on the Manchester Airport and East Didsbury lines will terminate at Firswood, and will not continue into Manchester city centre as normal The major works will begin on Monday, July 13, and continue for three weeks - with normal service expected to resume on Monday, August 3. It means that no trams will call at 31 Metrolink stops throughout those three weeks - including Deansgate-Castlefield and St Peter's Square in Trams will run between Bury and Ashton-under-Lyne, via Piccadilly, in both directions during the three-week period. All trams on the Rochdale line will terminate at Exchange Square Frequent tram replacement bus services will run between Altrincham and Piccadilly, Chorlton and Piccadilly, Eccles and Piccadilly, and the Trafford Centre and Piccadilly. Those services will call at every stop on their respective lines except Pomona

Styal Prison Prison officer brushed it off as a migraine, then she woke up to 'terrifying' experience Prison officer Tia Bradbury returned to the frontline at HMP Styal after undergoing brain surgery Comments News Amy Walker Court reporter 05:33, 06 J When Tia Bradbury started experiencing a headache and had difficulty seeing at 25, she brushed it off as just another migraine. She had no idea it was a sign of something much more sinister At the time, Tia’s eldest child was just one year old – and she was 22 weeks pregnant with her second. Then the young mother woke up one morning to find the vision in one of her eyes had disappeared completely "It was terrifying," the now 27-year-old recalls "I suffered from migraines, so at first I thought it was just another one,” she said. “But my sight didn’t come back. One morning I woke up and I couldn’t see out of one eye at all." After visits to an optician and hospital, scans revealed the problem - a 3cm brain tumour

Rochdale 'I was terrified as my rapist ex tried to appeal his conviction, but now I'm free' 'For anybody reading this and thinking they aren't strong enough to leave or to go through the legal process, you are' View 4 Images Speaking out: Hannah Mello News Paul Britton Reporter 05:32, 06 Jul 2026 A mum who was raped and abused by her then partner in a sickening campaign of violence and threats over years says she finally has justice Hannah Mellor spoke out after top judges at the Court of Appeal in London refused Ben Fitton's bid to appeal against his conviction and sentence The 33-year-old - who has bravely waived her right to anonymity to give hope to others in the same position she was in - told today how Fitton's appeal meant he was 'still in the shadows' of her life for 17 long months She admitted to being 'terrified' leave to appeal would be granted, but said the court's decision meant that, finally, she has peace Hannah revealed her mother travelled down to London to attend Thursday's appeal hearing in person, without her knowledge

Glossop Glossop homes evacuated and roads cordoned off after 'item dug up' in garden Multiple police vehicles were seen as roads in Glossop were taped off News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 20:06, 05 Jul 2026 Updated 21:44, 05 Jul Businesses and homes were reportedly evacuated with roads cordoned off amid what is understood to be an 'explosives'-related incident in Glossop on Sunday (July 5) Multiple police cars were seen along High Street West following the incident which is thought to have occurred nearby on Chapel Street. Witnesses reported that local residents along with nearby businesses were temporarily evacuated by emergency crews after the alarm was raised One man who found the item while gardening said the incident was escalated by emergency crews with explosives specialists also called out to the scene. Mental health An army career turned his life around - then it unravelled Gary Hind's anxiety led to physical pain that saw him physically floored News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 19:16, 05 Jul 2026 View Image Army veteran Gary Hind saw his life sp An army veteran whose military career 'turned his life around' was left physically floored as his anxiety spiralled. Gary Hind, from Hyde , lived with mental health difficulties from childhood following a troubled home situation and the sudden death of his father But the 66-year-old managed to get his life firmly on track with a career in the army. Gary joined the armed forces aged 16, and he credits the military for shaping the man he became In 1999, he received an MBE in recognition of his service. Gary went on to enjoy a new career, until events took a turn for the worse - leading to a mental health spiral that saw him suffer tightness in his chest, heart flutters and breathlessness After a difficult start in life, Gary says the army 'turned his life around'. He said: “Being in the army rebuilt me as a person. I thrived and learnt a lot of discipline and control. I felt like a new person.” When his military career came to an end, Gary found a job he loved helping students with their personal development skills and extra-curricular activities. But after 15 years in the role, a difficult period at work left him feeling unsettled and unsupported

World Cup Police stopping drivers across Greater Manchester after England v Mexico World Cup clash Punters were able to watch the World Cup match in pubs until 5am Comments News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 19:11, 05 Jul 2026 Upda Police have announced they are stopping drivers across Greater Manchester on Monday (July 6) following the early-hours England v Mexico World Cup clash The Three Lions beat Mexico 3-2 at the Azteca stadium in a game which kicked off at 2am UK time. It was announced last week that pubs would be able to stay open until 5am to host and stream the match to punters But in a new warning issued before the match, Greater Manchester Police said that officers would be conducting stops of vehicles across the region to check for any potential drug or drink driving offences after a night watching the match Due to the match taking place at the Azteca Stadium in Mexico City, the match has a later start time than other matches have done and it meant that its kick-off did not fall into the originally allowed rules of when pubs could extend their opening hours until And despite rumours FIFA might bring the kick off forward to 7pm on Sunday night, it was later confirmed kick off would remain at 1am on Monday morning - which was later put back to 2am due to thunderstorms

Salford Hundreds of people were meant to be living here by now - instead it looks like a 'bad day in Beirut' 'It's been abandoned and we've been abandoned' News Damon Wilkinson Reporter 18:42, 05 Jul 2026 View 2 Images The empty plot of land on Robert By now this desolate plot of wasteland should have been home to hundreds of people living on tree-lined streets on a brand new eco-friendly estate Instead it looks, in the words of one local resident, like a 'bad day in Beirut'. Questions are being asked over delays in the building of more than 270 homes in the Salford district of Ordsall in a scheme being managed by the council's development company Dérive The plans, which include 100 much-needed affordable houses in what is still one of the city's most deprived areas, were first approved in March 2024. It was hoped the 'vibrant, inclusive' estate's first residents would have moved in by now But more than two years on not a single brick has been laid. The two plots off Robert Hall Street were once home to a school and a shopping precinct But they have been fenced off and left to stand empty ever since they cleared of trees and groundworks carried out in the summer of 2024. And in the meantime residents say the site is becoming more and more of an eyesore

Chorlton 'If I'm going to do something this big, I want it to mean something beyond me' Toni wants the drastic change to shine a light on the community she strives to support Comments News Emma Gill Parents editor 17:05, 05 Jul 2026 View 4 Images Toni For Toni Toner her locks have been part of her identity for 14 years, so in deciding to go for 'the big chop' she was determined it would make a big difference As CEO for children and young people at Barlow Moor Community Association (BMCA) in Chorlton , she's spent years helping residents on the surrounding Merseybank Estate and Arrowfield Estate, formerly Nell Lane And while times have always been tough for many people there, she says in recent years, it's become harder than ever With demand for services soaring and voluntary sector funding harder to come by, she decided to do something drastic and have her long locks cut off to kickstart a summer of fundraising for BMCA and raise awareness of the key role such places play "The voluntary sector is really struggling; demand is at its highest while securing core funds has become increasingly difficult," said Toni, who lives in Sale Moor. "That reality is what pushed me to do something bold that could raise awareness as well as funds

Salford RSPCA responds after social media video shows dog chained up in Salford WARNING: This article contains images some readers may find upsetting News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 16:32, 05 Jul 2026 View 2 Images The RSPCA has The RSPCA has responded after a social media video appeared to show a dog trying to escape out of a chain in a yard in Salford . A clip, shared online, appeared to show the white dog trying to wriggle out of the collar with claims made that it was 'kept chained up at a scrapyard' A post by dog trainer and animal welfare campaigner Sammy Wilkinson received hundreds of responses and comments after it was shared on Thursday (July 2). "I've just watched with my own eyes this dog nearly choke himself to death trying to escape the chain," he wrote The clip, filmed in Swinton , zoomed in to show the animal appearing to be repeatedly struggling and wrestling with the collar and chain. It was claimed that the incident had been reported to the RSPCA However, a further update on social media then claimed that the owners had 'agreed to let the dog go if we can find a suitable home for him.' In a response, the RSPCA said it was 'aware of the situation' but could not comment on individual complaints or what action may or may not have been taken. It went on to encourage members of the public to report any other concerns about animals and their welfare

Wythenshawe Boy, 19, pulled from devastating house fire as mum issues update Joey Gregory was rushed to Wythenshawe Hospital and put into a medically-induced coma News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporter 16:08, 05 Jul 2026 Updated 17:11, A 19-year-old was rushed to a Manchester burns unit after being pulled from a devastating house fire. The 'catastrophic' blaze left 19-year-old Joey Gregory fighting for his life after suffering severe burns injuries in the incident The severe fire broke out at a house on Redcar Road in Blackpool on Tuesday, June 30, and also left a 13-year-old boy in a serious condition in hospital In an update posted online, Joey was said to have been taken to the burns intensive care unit at Wythenshawe Hospital, in south Manchester. In a post on Saturday (July 4), his mum posted Facebook: "We were told on Tuesday that the first 24-48 hours were critical. Then they said 7 "Keep fighting sunshine, we ain’t going nowhere. We are so lucky to have such amazing family & friends. My boy is so loved. I don’t know where we are finding the strength from. Please keep praying." Following the incident, a GoFundMe page was set up by Jane Richardson to support the family of Joey. It said how the teenager had undergone emergency treatment and was then placed in a medically induced coma

Bolton Biker fighting for life after serious Bolton crash as two men arrested Chaotic scenes erupted as police responded to the crash News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 12:39, 05 Jul 2026 Updated 12:49, 05 Jul 2026 View Image St Helens Road, Bolton A biker is fighting for his life following a horror crash on a busy Bolton road. Emergency services descended on St Helens Road at around 10.18pm on Saturday night (July 4) Greater Manchester Police says the collision involved an off-road bike and a car which was stationary. The rider remains in a critical condition in hospital this afternoon, according to GMP The Manchester Evening News understands that while officers worked at the scene, two men in the area hurled abusive language towards officers and refused to comply with their instructions. It's understood that multiple attempts were made to de-escalate the situation, including en A GMP spokesperson said: "At around 10:18pm yesterday evening (July 4), officers responded to reports of a collision involving an off-road bike and a stationary car on St Helens Road, Bolton. Officers were already in the area at the time of the collision and were quickly on scene "The rider suffered serious injuries and was taken to hospital, where he remains in a critical condition. Two men were arrested at the scene on suspicion of obstructing police while officers attempted to assist the injured male."

Wigan Reform councillor in Wigan under investigation over social media posts calling for politicians to be 'shot' The alleged posts made by the Reform councillor in Wigan included references to shootings and hangings News Charlotte Hall Local Democrac A Reform councillor in Wigan is ‘under investigation’ by his own party after social media posts resurfaced which appear to show him calling for former politicians to ‘be shot’ Councillor Adrian White allegedly also shared posts supporting far-right extremist Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, aka Tommy Robinson, and posts peddling conspiracy theories about Covid-19 and climate change. The representative for Tyldesley and Mosley won his seat with almost 45 per cent Now campaign group Hope Not Hate claim to have uncovered several posts between 2019 and 2025 that they’ve described as ‘awful and bizarre’. At the time of writing, the posts are publicly visible on a personal profile on Facebook, where coun White has also shared his councillor de One 2018 post on the account features an image of former Conservative cabinet member and House of Lords member Michael Heseltine, with the caption: “Lord Haw Haw… Shoot him and his fellow parasites! Fill yer pockets… Dirty money!” A year later, when calls were being made for then Prime Minister Theresa May to resign, another post reads: “Champagne back on ice again… can’t we just shoot her… please.”

Manchester weather Met Office latest on when another heatwave will hit Greater Manchester It's going to be hot once again News Stephen Topping Senior reporter 15:23, 05 Jul 2026 View Image Another heatwave is on the way (Image: Met Office) If you're glad to be cooling down after the last heatwave , brace yourself for what lies ahead this week. Another heatwave is hitting the UK - and it is due in Greater Manchester before next weekend The Met Office is forecasting plenty of sunshine across the region this week. Temperatures will be more mild at the beginning of the week, before ramping up as the days go on Highs of 22C are currently forecast on Monday (July 6) and Tuesday. An overcast start to Monday should make way for sunny intervals from late morning, continuing throughout the rest of the day, with Tuesday following a similar pattern While some parts of the country are already experiencing another heatwave at the start of the week, Greater Manchester is due to warm up from Wednesday. Highs of 27C are forecast for Wednesday, with 29C on both Thursday and Friday, and 28C on Saturday - with clear skies forecast Overnight temperatures will be above average throughout the week, although they are thankfully not expected to reach 20C like in the June heatwave. Lows of between 14C and 16C are forecast over the next three nights, while they could reach 18C later in the week

Stockport The derelict Stockport mill that has seen 'illegal raves' but could now get new lease of life Compstall Mill helped build the area around it but today is only home to pigeons. However there is new hope the site could be brought back to life Nestled away in a quiet Stockport village, an abandoned mill could be given a new lease of life. Compstall Mill helped build the area around it and once housed cotton mill workers but today is only home to pigeons The building was built in the 1830s with the nearby village of Compstall built by businessman George Andrew to house his 800 workers at the mills. Manchester Nine pieces of Greater Manchester history at risk of being lost forever Pubs, an old bank, and an elegant shopping mall are on a national at risk list Comments News Neal Keeling Chief reporter 07:32, 05 Jul 2026 View 13 Images A once much-l There are nine new Greater Manchester entries on the SAVE Britain's Heritage Buildings At Risk Register. Also identified as in danger of being lost forever are a Georgian canal warehouse; a "striking" red-bricked factory in Trafford ; a former bank; Victorian solicitors offices; One of them is Barton Arcade, which sits between Deansgate and St Ann's Square in Manchester city centre. It was completed in 1871 by the firm Corbett, Raby & Sawyer and was inspired by iconic European destinations such as Milan’s Galleria Vittorio Emanuele II and Sir Joseph Paxt It stretches 226 feet wide and 53 feet tall under striking glass and iron roof and is named for the developer, John Hope Barton Among its earliest and most celebrated tenants was Stephenson’s Glass & Chinaware, a retailer known for elegant ceramics and fine household goods, which remained a fixture of the arcade for nearly 100 years, until 1967 But the arcade was not exclusively for shops. In the late 1870s, it was home to the Humane Society for the Hundred of Salford , which provided emergency rescue equipment for canal workers. In the 1880s, a phrenologist, practising the art of reading skull shapes, set up shop here

As America celebrates its 250th Birthday this weekend we take a look at what effect the momentous events over the water played in Manchester Manchester as far as we are aware played no part in the American Declaration of Independence but its consequences would have in the future profound effects on the growing town Manchester at the time of the Declaration of Independence had a population of around 25,000, it would double in the next twenty years When news arrived in the town of the crisis across the Atlantic, the town was supportive of the King’s response A meeting at the Bull’s Head backed the response of George III and “looked on with horror at this unnatural war” and were treat to support the king in any measures needed to suppress the rebellion in the colonies However the mood would change. Purchased homes will also be made available quickly. Every penny of our rental income will get reinvested, which is not the case f A Green mayor of Greater Manchester will end Labour’s developer-led rip off by setting up a publicly owned provider, Homes for Greater Manchester, to take housing back from big money This new body will combine building new low-carbon homes and using a buy, retrofit, let model to quickly bring empty and derelict homes back into use By contrast, Labour’s model for Greater Manchester has been to hand over large sums of public money to developers in return for little to none affordable housing

Wigan Reform councillor in Wigan under investigation over social media posts calling for politicians to be 'shot' The alleged posts made by the Reform councillor in Wigan included references to shootings and hangings News Charlotte Hall Local Democrac A Reform councillor in Wigan is ‘under investigation’ by his own party after social media posts resurfaced which appear to show him calling for former politicians to ‘be shot’ Councillor Adrian White allegedly also shared posts supporting far-right extremist Stephen Yaxley-Lennon, aka Tommy Robinson, and posts peddling conspiracy theories about Covid-19 and climate change. title: Major disruption to Metrolink services for £150m upgrade works
summary: Trams services in Greater Manchester are out of action on three lines for the next three weeks as part of a major £150m upgrade Tracks are being replaced between Trafford Bar, Cornbrook and Deansgate-Castlefield with the work starting on 13 July and running until 2 August Transpo
lead: Trams services in Greater Manchester are out of action on three lines for the next three weeks as part of a major £150m upgrade Tracks are being replaced between Trafford Bar, Cornbrook and Deansgate-Castlefield with the work starting on 13 July and running until 2 August Transpo

More than a third of under-threat theatres in the UK are in the north west of England, a heritage charity has revealed Theatres Trust, the national advice and advocacy body for the UK's theatres, has revealed the 39 venues at risk in this year's register, with 14 in Greater Manchester, Lancashire and Merseyside It said these are the theatres across the UK "threatened by a risk of closure, redevelopment or severe decay, but which had potential to be revived with the right support" One of the latest additions to the register is Preston Guild Hall which opened in 1972, along with the The Forum in Barrow Preston Guild Hall complex comprises of the Grand Hall which has seen legendary acts play on stage from Led Zeppelin to The Jam - it used to host the UK Snooker Championships and also situated is the smaller Charter Theatre Preston's only professional theatre has been closed for seven years initially due to legal disputes but latterly due to Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (RAAC) - the so-called crumbling concrete - in the roof

A mother has described how a family day out at a country park ended in a "terrifying" road rage attack which left her children in tears The Liverpool family had just left an American classic car show at Tatton Park in Knutsford, Cheshire, when a man confronted her after she did not pull over to let him drive past Dashcam footage of the incident, at 15:00 BST on Saturday, shows him braking in front of the family's car before climbing out of his van. He can then be heard swearing, before he rips the car door handle off Portable toilet firm Instant Loos and Event Hire has apologised and said its subcontractor would no longer work for them. Cheshire Police said it was investigating the incident The mother, Olivia, was in the car with her two six-month-old babies and a 13-year-old She told the BBC she was "absolutely devastated by what my family went through"

A government minister has said they were open to changing the law to deport Shabir Ahmed, the freed ringleader of a Rochdale grooming gang that targeted girls as young as 12 Ahmed, 73, who was known as 'Daddy' by his victims, was jailed for 22 years in August 2012 for a raft of child sexual offences including rape but released on licence last week Alex Norris Home Office minster of state told the Commons the government was "examining every option" and reiterated Ahmed could not be deported unless a 55-year-old law was amended Norris was responding to an Urgent Question in the house by Conservative MP Katie Lam Lam said the idea that Ahmed could not be deported due to a "decades-old law is as just absurd as it is sickening" She said as recently as 2023 parole officers said Ahmed "poses a very high risk of harm to children"

Soon after, Germane Tarrant was in handcuffs. He was cornered, with nowhere to escape It was the culmination of a covert investigation into Tarrant and his gang, which flooded the streets of Sale and Altrincham with drugs. Tarrant is now serving the first few nights of a near decade long prison sentence Tarrant and four others were sentenced at Minshull Street Crown Court on Monday (July 6). Through Tarrant, his partner Letitia Sandys, 31, became involved in assisting his crimes Tarrant’s personal trainer at the gym, Nicholas Griffin, 39, also became involved and was locked up. Greater Manchester mayoral election 2026 Green mayoral candidate promises high streets 'task force' to take over empty shops Green mayoral candidate Geraldine Coggins says she would use compulsory purchase powers to bring long-empty shops back into us Ms Coggins says she will use Compulsory Purchase Orders (CPOs) to take over empty properties if she becomes the city-region’s next leader The academic has promised to set up an Empty Shops Team to co-ordinate CPOs through Town Centre Partnership Agreements. Greater Manchester mayoral election 2026 'I'm horrified by the prospect of a Reform mayor': Greens' candidate Geraldine Coggins on why she's running for mayor Geraldine Coggins says Labour has left Greater Manchester vulnerable to Reform and believes Uninspired by the three main established parties, she joined the Green Party in the hope of “making a difference”. “By the time the next general election came around in 2015, I was able to vote Green because I voted for myself,” she says. She has since stood for the Greens in eac “I just saw things that needed doing. Greater Manchester mayoral election 2026 Why George Galloway isn't standing to replace Andy Burnham in the Greater Manchester Mayoral by-election The Workers Party leader declared he would throw his hat in the ring to replace Andy Burnham as Greater M George Galloway will not be standing to replace Andy Burnham as the mayor for Greater Manchester, despite saying he was going to Last month the leader of the Workers Party said he would be standing to replace Mr Burnham, who stepped down from his role after successfully winning the Makerfield by-election However, Mr Galloway’s candidacy in the mayoral by-election was dependent on assurances from the government he would not be detained again The former Celebrity Big Brother contestant is currently living outside of the UK in self-imposed exile after being held at Gatwick Airport by counter-terrorism police in September The Workers Party say they did not receive these assurances and believe there could be an incoming general election. The political party did not field a candidate at all in the Greater Manchester mayoral by-election being fought on July 30

Before her extraordinary transformation, Lyndsey Kelly would eat takeaways at least once a week, drink every weekend and rarely exercise But when the mum-of-one reached her mid 40s, she decided it was time to turn her life around. The 47-year-old hired a personal trainer and started waking up as early as 4am for workouts before heading off for 12-shifts as a machine operator Now, Lyndsey has lost nearly five stone by doing 40 minutes of fasted cardio at 4am five times a week and eating the same meals every single day She has found a love for weightlifting and now at 8st 8lbs she competes in bodybuilding competition “I work 12 hour shifts so I get up at 4am and do 40 minutes of fasted cardio, then I walk to work and do a weights session in the gym after my shift finishes," she said

Salford Salford man, 20, fighting for life after horror quad bike crash on lads' holiday to Zante Bradley had flown out to the Greek island with friends the day before disaster struck, his mum said News James Holt Senior Live and Breaking News Reporte The family of a 20-year-old man have issued a desperate plea for help after he was left fighting for his life following a quad bike crash. The party had rented quads to ride on on the Greek island when disaster struck and he was involved in the serious crash while riding with another friend, who was also hospitalised The 20-year-old, who is 'loved by everyone' is now in intensive care and in a coma after being transported to a hospital in Attica, in Athens, mainland Greece, his family have said Speaking to the Manchester Evening News, his mum Leanne Rabbetts said: "He went on the holiday on Thursday and everything was fine. I spoke to him when he arrived, but then on the Friday afternoon I got the call saying there had been a crash "I was thinking 'what the hell happened?'. Further survivors were then uncovered who supported the investigation and brought O'Rourke to justice During her victim impact statement read to court, one survivor said: "I stand here today to finish giving that child you tried to destroy, her voice, and end all the hurt you caused. You made me feel so dirty and embarrassed for so many years

Mr Bell, who in the previous three months had no history of mental health iss But there was no handover to staff and guards on his new wing didn't know he was on the DNU list. The cutting from the MEN on the Lottery chiefs response in 2002 is on the wall of The Smiths Room at the red-bricked Edward The building attracts Smiths fans from all over the world after the band were photographed on the steps of the entrance by Stephen Wright for the original promo on the cover of the 1987 single Stop Me If You've Heard This One Before. His pictures were also used on the inner sleev In 2002 the club were seeking £400,000 to finance new toilets, central heating, and roof repairs. Greater Manchester has spent the past few years turning itself into one of the UK’s busiest remote and hybrid-work economies, from the digital and creative firms clustered around the city centre to the professional-services teams spread across the region. New research suggests th A Censuswide survey of 4,000 remote workers across the UK, Germany, Italy and Spain, commissioned by the remote-first company iGaming.com, found that just 7.8% take a proper sick day and fully switch off when unwell. In the UK, 51.4% say they now work through illness more than th The driver is less about attitude than economics. UK workers are covered by Statutory Sick Pay of around £118 a week during the survey period, far below the full-pay systems in countries such as Germany, where workers proved the least likely of all to work while ill. For many Gre It would be easy to read fewer sick days as a healthier workforce. The national data says otherwise. The latest ONS figures still record around 4.4 sick days lost per worker and a sickness absence rate near 2%, with millions of working days lost each year. People are not getting The strain is sharpest among younger workers, who make up a large share of Greater Manchester’s digital and creative workforce. Across the survey, 26% of Gen Z said they work from bed when ill, and they were the most likely generation to report loneliness and trouble switching of For the region’s employers, many of them growing, this is a productivity issue as much as a wellbeing one. Greater Manchester Police seized over £1 million in suspected criminal cash during a week of action tackling organised immigration crime and criminality on local high streets Operation Lockstream week of action is a national partnership initiative led by the Organised Immigration Crime Domestic Taskforce (OICDT) at the Regional Organised Crime Unit (ROCU) to tackle organised immigration crime within the service sector in local communities The intensification week ran from Monday 15 to Sunday 21 June 2026, with GMP having the highest number of districts taking part across all participating forces in the country On Monday 15 June, the City of Manchester (CoM) Central and South District Intelligence Unit (DIU), alongside neighbourhood officers and partnership agencies, carried out a number of visits in the area to tackle criminality on the local high street During the first visit to a car wash, eight men fled upon sight of the team approaching. raw_evidence: The Public Health team and partners in Bolton are urging residents to think about how their drinking can impact on their health and wellbeing, and their lives as part of Alcohol Awareness Week This year's Alcohol Awareness Week , takes place from 6-12 July and its theme is 'Alcohol and Me' The campaign is led by Alcohol Change UK , a UK charity who wants to create a society free from the physical, mental, societal, and economic harms associated with alcohol To help promote the campaign the charity is sharing In a world where alcohol is often made to feel central to everyday moments, from birthdays and barbecues to nights in front of the TV and celebrations with friends, this year’s campaign invites us to pause and reflect on the role that alcohol is playing in our lives Around 10 million of us are regularly drinking alcohol in ways that can harm our health and wellbeing

Albert Halls Bolton is serving up a uniquely local experience this July, with a special Phoenix Nights–themed pre-show dining offer running alongside Peter Kay’s sold-out hometown shows Taking place inside The Albert bar & restaurant, the themed dining experience invites audiences to start their evening in true Bolton style; celebrating the humour, heart, and nostalgia of the much-loved TV series, as well as Peter Kay’s iconic food observations, before heading i Designed to complement the excitement of Peter Kay’s return, the experience blends familiar flavours with playful nods to Phoenix Nights , making it the perfect addition to an already unforgettable night The specially created menu, named Tonight, at the Phoenix pays tribute to the spirit of Peter Kay and his hit show, with a selection of dishes inspired by memorable moments and Northern charm. Highlights include Thursday 9 July, Friday 10 July, Saturday 11 July evenings: 4pm – 6:30pm Saturday 11 July Matinee: 12pm – 2:15pm (75 mins per table) The booking form and full menu details are available here: Peter Kay Live in Bolton- Pre-show information | Albert Halls

Crime Preston Davey murderer Jamie Varley's neighbour reported him to police after 'nightmare' incidents WARNING, DISTRESSING CONTENT: The neighbour described the child killer as 'horrible' as she spoke out News Jon Macpherson , Liam Randall and Ashli A former neighbour has described the disturbing warning signs shown by former schoolteacher Jamie Varley years before he sexually abused and murdered his adopted baby boy The neighbour described Varley's household as a 'nightmare' and said he was 'horrible' to live opposite. Varley, 37, who lived in Grimsargh in Lancashire, is currently serving a whole-life order for the horrific killing of 13-month-old Preston Davey On the day of his tragic death on July 27, 2023, defenceless Preston endured two unspeakable sexual assaults at the hands of the secondary school teacher. Varley had climbed the professional ladder to become a head of year and safeguarding lead at South Shore Academy in Blackpool His partner, former public schoolboy John McGowan-Fazakerley, meanwhile, was employed as an accounts manager for a finance firm. Yet beneath this respectable façade lay a deeply disturbing past In sharp contrast to his partner's privileged, middle-class upbringing, Varley was raised on a council estate in Newark
